label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out if following these instructions brings me back to the starting point. Let me go step by step. First, I need to visualize my movement. Let's assume I start at the origin point (0,0). I'm facing forward, which I'll take as the positive y-direction (up). 1. Take 5 steps forward. Since I'm facing forward (up), each step moves me +1 in the y-direction. So after 5 steps, I'm at (0, 5). 2. Take 8 steps backward. Backward relative to my current direction, which is still forward (up). So moving backward would be in the negative y-direction. Each step is -1 in y. 8 steps back from (0,5) would be 5 - 8 = -3. So now I'm at (0, -3). 3. Take 4 steps forward. Again, facing the same direction (up). So moving forward again, +4 in y. Starting from (0, -3), adding 4 to y gives (0, 1). 4. Take 4 steps right. Now, "right" direction depends on which way I'm facing. I'm still facing forward (up), so right would be the positive x-direction. Moving 4 steps to the right adds 4 to the x-coordinate. So from (0,1), moving right 4 steps takes me to (4,1). Now, my final position is (4,1), while the starting point was (0,0). Since (4,1) ≠ (0,0), I haven't returned to the starting point. Therefore, the answer should be "No." Wait, but maybe I should check if I changed direction at any point? The first instruction says "Always face forward." So I don't turn during any steps. So I maintain the same direction throughout. So my direction doesn't change, so all forward/backward are along
